subtopic_detail: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts in a child
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 5-year-old girl referred from her GP with a 3-week history of progressive pallor, recurrent low-grade fevers, easy bruising on her shins and a new petechial rash on her trunk noticed by her mother yesterday. She has been more tired than usual at preschool and has refused to walk to the car last week, complaining of left thigh ache. On examination she is pale, has scattered petechiae and small ecchymoses, a soft 3 cm spleen tip palpable, and mild bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 degrees C, HR 122, RR 24, BP 96/58 mmHg, SpO2 99% air
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, scattered petechiae and bruises, soft spleen tip 3 cm, no hepatomegaly, no testicular masses, no neurological signs
data_table.Investigations: Hb 64 g/L, MCV 84 fL, WCC 28.4 x10^9/L (neutrophils 0.6, blasts 22.0 x10^9/L), platelets 22 x10^9/L, LDH 1820 U/L, urate 0.48 mmol/L, K 4.4, phosphate 1.62, calcium corrected 2.18

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Arrange an urgent CT abdomen and pelvis to confirm splenomegaly and exclude an intra-abdominal lymphoma mass, plus a peripheral lymph node excisional biopsy for histopathology before considering systemic chemotherapy or transfer
options.rationale: Excisional lymph node biopsy is appropriate for suspected lymphoma without circulating blasts; here marrow examination is faster and definitive. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL - Induction)
source_refs: eviQ: Paediatric ALL - Induction

options.text: Admit to a regional paediatric ward for transfusion of one adult unit of packed red blood cells and one adult therapeutic dose of platelets, then refer to the haematology outpatient clinic for a bone marrow biopsy within the next two weeks
options.rationale: Transfusion without first taking diagnostic marrow may confound flow cytometry; outpatient delay risks progressive cytopenia and tumour lysis. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL - Induction)
source_refs: eviQ: Paediatric ALL - Induction

options.text: Start empirical intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin for presumed febrile neutropenia and oral fluconazole for fungal prophylaxis, and arrange a CT chest abdomen and pelvis to look for a sanctuary site of infection or occult abscess
options.rationale: Although febrile neutropenia management is needed if a fever spikes, the immediate priority is diagnosis with bone marrow aspirate and tumour lysis prophylaxis; abx are not first-line in stable apyrexial neutropenia. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL - Induction)
source_refs: eviQ: Paediatric ALL - Induction

options.text: Transfer urgently to a tertiary paediatric oncology unit for bone marrow aspirate and trephine plus immunophenotyping and cytogenetics, and commence intravenous hyperhydration with allopurinol or rasburicase for tumour lysis prophylaxis en route
options.rationale: Circulating blasts on film with pancytopenia, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy and raised LDH require urgent transfer for diagnostic bone marrow plus tumour lysis prophylaxis given the WCC and urate; ALL is the most likely diagnosis. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL - Induction)
source_refs: eviQ: Paediatric ALL - Induction

options.text: Send blood for Epstein Barr virus, cytomegalovirus and parvovirus B19 serology and a peripheral blood film for atypical lymphocytes, and review again in 48 hours once infective causes of pancytopenia have been excluded by these results
options.rationale: Viral serology has a place but never delays urgent tertiary referral when circulating blasts and a leukaemoid white cell count are present. (Source: eviQ: Paediatric ALL - Induction)
source_refs: eviQ: Paediatric ALL - Induction

explanation.summary: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is the most common childhood malignancy, typically presenting between 2 and 5 years with marrow failure (anaemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), bone pain, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy and constitutional symptoms. Circulating blasts on film are diagnostic until proven otherwise. Tumour lysis risk increases with WCC over 50, raised LDH, urate over 0.45 mmol/L and bulky disease; intravenous hyperhydration at twice maintenance plus allopurinol (or rasburicase if very high risk) is started before induction. Tertiary transfer for marrow aspirate, immunophenotyping and minimal residual disease assessment guides risk-stratified chemotherapy.
key_points: Triad: marrow failure + organomegaly + bone pain in pre-school child
Film: circulating blasts trigger urgent transfer
Tumour lysis: hydrate twice-maintenance plus allopurinol or rasburicase
Avoid transfusion before diagnostic marrow if possible
Australian protocols via eviQ; cure rate over 90 percent in standard-risk B-ALL
explanation.pearls: Mediastinal mass on chest X-ray in T-cell ALL can compress trachea or great vessels; lying flat for procedures becomes hazardous and dictates early imaging.

sources.label: Children's Cancer and Leukaemia Group: ALL guidance

subtopic_detail: IgA vasculitis with significant proteinuria requiring biopsy decision
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 9-year-old boy who was diagnosed in your clinic five weeks ago with IgA vasculitis based on a palpable purpuric rash over his lower limbs and buttocks, bilateral ankle arthralgia and intermittent peri-umbilical pain. The rash has now resolved. You arranged weekly urine dipstick and blood pressure at his GP. Today his GP rings because his urinalysis shows 3+ protein with 2+ blood and his blood pressure is 130/86 mmHg seated. He looks well, is afebrile and has no oedema on examination today in your clinic.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 130/86 mmHg (above the 95th centile for age, sex, height), HR 84, T 36.6 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Faded post-inflammatory pigmentation on lower limbs, no fresh purpura, no oedema, abdomen soft, no joint swelling
data_table.Investigations: Urine PCR 220 mg/mmol, urine RBC 60/high power field, dysmorphic, Hb 124 g/L, creatinine 68 micromol/L, eGFR 95, albumin 36 g/L, complement C3 and C4 normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure that microscopic haematuria and mild proteinuria are expected after Henoch-Schonlein purpura and may persist for over a year without need for intervention, and continue weekly urinalysis monitoring with the general practitioner
options.rationale: Trace haematuria alone may resolve, but PCR over 100 mg/mmol with hypertension represents progressive nephritis that requires nephrology input rather than continued primary care monitoring. (Source: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis)
source_refs: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis

options.text: Refer to a paediatric nephrologist for an urgent renal biopsy and consideration of oral prednisolone or pulse methylprednisolone with adjunctive ACE inhibitor and blood pressure control as guided by biopsy ISKDC class
options.rationale: Persistent heavy proteinuria (PCR over 250 mg/mmol or nephrotic-range) with hypertension at 4 weeks after IgA vasculitis onset triggers nephrology referral for biopsy; ISKDC class II to V guides steroid plus immunosuppression decisions. (Source: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis)
source_refs: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis

options.text: Start a 4-week course of oral prednisolone at 1 mg/kg/day without nephrology referral and aim to taper over a further 4 weeks while continuing community-based monitoring of urinalysis and blood pressure throughout the taper
options.rationale: Empirical steroid without biopsy classification is not first-line for HSP nephritis at this severity; KDIGO and RCH guide biopsy-stratified therapy rather than blind community steroid courses. (Source: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis)
source_refs: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis

options.text: Commence oral cyclophosphamide and mycophenolate mofetil empirically as combination therapy and arrange repeat urine protein creatinine ratio in 4 weeks to demonstrate biochemical response before deciding on need for renal biopsy
options.rationale: Cyclophosphamide is considered only after biopsy demonstrates crescentic or class IV-V disease and after steroid trial; empirical use is inappropriate. (Source: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis)
source_refs: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis

options.text: Test antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody, anti-GBM antibody and serum cryoglobulins urgently and treat as ANCA-associated small vessel vasculitis with plasma exchange and rituximab pending these confirmatory serological results
options.rationale: Clinical picture of preceding palpable purpura with normal complement is classical IgA vasculitis nephritis, not ANCA-associated vasculitis; serology should be confirmatory but not delay biopsy referral. (Source: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis)
source_refs: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis

explanation.summary: IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schonlein purpura) is a small-vessel leukocytoclastic vasculitis of children with palpable purpura, arthritis, abdominal pain and nephritis. Renal involvement determines long-term prognosis. Australian and KDIGO pathways monitor blood pressure and urinalysis weekly for 4 weeks then monthly to 6 months. Indications for nephrology referral and biopsy include nephrotic-range proteinuria, persistent PCR over 100 to 250 mg/mmol, hypertension, raised creatinine or AKI, or atypical features. Biopsy ISKDC class guides treatment: class II-IIIA may have RAAS blockade alone; IIIB+ get steroids; class IV-V and crescentic disease often need pulse methylprednisolone plus cyclophosphamide or MMF.
key_points: Monitor urine and BP weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly to 6 months after diagnosis
PCR over 100 to 250 mg/mmol, hypertension, AKI or nephrotic syndrome trigger nephrology referral
Biopsy stratifies treatment per ISKDC class
RAAS blockade is the cornerstone of proteinuria control
Long-term follow up because late progression to CKD can occur
explanation.pearls: Normal complement is the rule in IgA vasculitis nephritis - low complement should redirect workup toward post-infectious glomerulonephritis or lupus nephritis instead.

sources.label: KDIGO 2021: Glomerular Diseases - IgA Vasculitis Nephritis

subtopic_detail: Mitochondrial disease presenting as developmental regression with lactic acidosis
stem: You are the paediatric registrar consulted on a 16-month-old boy admitted with two days of vomiting and decreased oral intake after a febrile illness. He was born at term, breastfed and weaned uneventfully but his parents now describe loss of words he previously had, loss of ability to walk in the last month and intermittent eye movement abnormalities. On examination he is thin but not dehydrated, has truncal hypotonia, brisk reflexes, an upgoing right plantar response and a left ptosis. There is no organomegaly. He has had no surgeries and his immunisations are up to date.

data_table.Examination: Hypotonia, brisk reflexes, left ptosis, intermittent dysconjugate eye movements, no organomegaly, weight on 3rd centile
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.28, lactate 6.8 mmol/L, glucose 4.0, K 3.8, Na 136, anion gap 22; CK 220 U/L; LFTs ALT 78 AST 102; ammonia 92 micromol/L; MRI brain shows symmetric bilateral basal ganglia and brainstem T2 hyperintensities

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Send blood and urine for very-long-chain fatty acids and white-cell enzyme analysis for late-infantile metachromatic leukodystrophy, and discharge home pending results because the child is haemodynamically stable and feeding adequately
options.rationale: MLD is a leukodystrophy with white matter lesions, not symmetric grey-matter basal ganglia pattern; discharge before treatment risks metabolic decompensation with intercurrent illness. (Source: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline)
source_refs: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline

options.text: Treat empirically as autoimmune encephalitis with intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day for 5 days followed by intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg, and arrange anti-NMDA and anti-MOG antibody testing on serum and cerebrospinal fluid
options.rationale: Autoimmune encephalitis is unlikely with symmetric metabolic-pattern lesions on MRI and persistent lactic acidosis; the clinical and imaging pattern points to mitochondrial disease. (Source: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline)
source_refs: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline

options.text: Manage as severe gastroenteritis with intravenous sodium chloride 0.9% at 1.5 times maintenance plus ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg orally and continue ad lib oral feeding once vomiting subsides without further metabolic workup or imaging review
options.rationale: Gastroenteritis would not produce a persistent lactic acidosis with developmental regression, ptosis and basal ganglia lesions; the presentation is metabolic. (Source: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline)
source_refs: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline

options.text: Start sodium valproate orally as empirical treatment for suspected refractory partial seizures presenting as dysconjugate eye movements, and arrange outpatient electroencephalography with sleep deprivation within the next fortnight
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is contraindicated in suspected mitochondrial disease (especially POLG-related) due to risk of fatal hepatotoxicity; ictal-eye movements should not be empirically treated this way. (Source: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline)
source_refs: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline

options.text: Refer urgently to a metabolic genetics service for whole-exome sequencing and mitochondrial DNA testing, commence high-dose riboflavin, thiamine and coenzyme Q10 cofactor therapy, and avoid sodium valproate, metformin and aminoglycosides during admission
options.rationale: Symmetric basal ganglia and brainstem T2 lesions with lactic acidosis and a hypotonic regression suggest Leigh syndrome or mitochondrial disease; tertiary referral, mitochondrial cofactor cocktail and avoidance of mitochondrial-toxic drugs are recommended pending genetic diagnosis. (Source: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline)
source_refs: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline

explanation.summary: Mitochondrial disease presents at any age but in infancy and toddlers classically features developmental regression, hypotonia, lactic acidosis, multisystem involvement (cardiomyopathy, deafness, retinopathy, hepatic and renal tubular dysfunction) and characteristic MRI patterns. Leigh syndrome shows symmetric T2 hyperintensities in basal ganglia, brainstem and cerebellum with raised brain or CSF lactate. Initial management is referral to tertiary metabolic genetics, mitochondrial cofactor support (riboflavin, thiamine, coenzyme Q10, biotin, levocarnitine) and strict avoidance of mitochondrial toxins including sodium valproate, metformin, linezolid, aminoglycosides and propofol infusions. Genetic diagnosis via mitochondrial DNA panel plus nuclear gene exome sequencing guides prognosis and family counselling.
key_points: Symmetric basal ganglia lesions + lactic acidosis + regression = Leigh syndrome until proven otherwise
Cofactor cocktail: riboflavin, thiamine, CoQ10, levocarnitine, biotin
Avoid valproate (fatal hepatotoxicity in POLG), metformin, aminoglycosides
Genetic confirmation by mtDNA panel + nuclear WES
Multidisciplinary care: neurology, cardiology, ophthalmology, audiology, dietetics
explanation.pearls: POLG mutations cause Alpers syndrome and are exquisitely sensitive to sodium valproate, which can precipitate fulminant hepatic failure - a key reason mitochondrial disease must be excluded before valproate in early-life epilepsy.

sources.label: HUMAN: Genetics and Metabolic - Mitochondrial disease guideline

subtopic_detail: Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis indications and antibiotic choice
stem: You are the obstetric resident on the labour ward reviewing a 28-year-old G2P1 at 39+1 weeks who presented 6 hours ago in spontaneous labour. Her routine 36-week vaginal-rectal swab grew Group B streptococcus. She is in established labour with regular contractions every 3 minutes and is now 6 cm dilated with intact membranes. Her temperature is 37.0, fetal heart rate is reassuring on intermittent auscultation, and she reports no penicillin allergy. Her previous baby was unaffected by GBS.

data_table.Examination: 6 cm cervix, fully effaced, vertex at -1 station, membranes intact, regular contractions q3 min
data_table.Investigations: 36-week LVS positive for GBS; FBC and routine antenatal bloods normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic plan?

options.text: Administer a single oral dose of 1 g azithromycin in early labour as effective single-dose prophylaxis covering Group B streptococcus and atypical organisms, with no further antibiotic required throughout the labour course
options.rationale: Azithromycin is not an accepted intrapartum prophylactic agent for GBS; macrolides do not reliably eradicate maternal colonisation or prevent vertical transmission. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: Defer antibiotic administration until membrane rupture or until the cervix reaches 8 cm because GBS transmission risk only becomes clinically relevant in the second stage of labour with imminent delivery imminent
options.rationale: IAP must begin at least 4 hours before delivery to ensure adequate placental and fetal levels; waiting until 8 cm or after rupture risks insufficient coverage. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: Commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g now followed by 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery to provide adequate intrapartum antibiotic cover for early-onset neonatal GBS sepsis prevention
options.rationale: RANZCOG and KEMH protocols give benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours from labour onset or rupture of membranes until birth in penicillin non-allergic women colonised with GBS. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: Commence intravenous vancomycin 1 g over one hour every 12 hours as first-line therapy because vancomycin has superior intrapartum tissue penetration and lower neonatal nephrotoxicity than narrow-spectrum penicillins given in labour
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy with cefazolin contraindicated and clindamycin-resistant GBS; it is not first-line in a non-allergic woman. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: Decline intrapartum prophylaxis because she had a healthy unaffected first baby and previously unaffected babies have established maternal immunity that durably reduces transmission risk to less than one percent during any subsequent labour
options.rationale: Previous unaffected baby is not protective against GBS transmission in subsequent pregnancies if maternal colonisation is now positive; IAP is still indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Australian intrapartum GBS prophylaxis follows a screening approach with universal vaginal-rectal swab at 35 to 37 weeks. Positive carriers receive IAP in labour from labour onset or rupture of membranes. First-line is IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery. Penicillin allergy with low anaphylaxis risk: cefazolin 2 g loading then 1 g every 8 hours. High-risk penicillin allergy: clindamycin 900 mg every 8 hours if susceptible, otherwise vancomycin. Risk-factor-based prophylaxis (no swab, preterm, ROM over 18 h, fever, previous GBS infant) is also used when swab unavailable.
key_points: Universal vaginal-rectal swab at 35-37 weeks
Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g q4h until birth (first-line)
Cefazolin 2 g then 1 g q8h for mild penicillin allergy
Clindamycin or vancomycin for severe penicillin allergy
IAP started at least 4 hours before delivery for full benefit
explanation.pearls: Previous infant with invasive GBS disease is itself an automatic indication for IAP in this pregnancy without screening - that risk persists despite negative current swab.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

stem:
You are the antenatal care GP reviewing a 32-year-old G1P0 at 27+5 weeks. She has a BMI of 31, her mother has type 2 diabetes and she is otherwise well. Her routine 28-week 75 g oral glucose tolerance test taken this week returned a fasting plasma glucose of 5.3 mmol/L, a 1-hour value of 9.8 mmol/L and a 2-hour value of 8.2 mmol/L.

She has had no symptoms of polyuria or polydipsia. Her fundal height is 28 cm and the fetus is moving well; recent ultrasound at 22 weeks was unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78, HR 80, T 36.6, weight 86 kg, BMI 31
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, fundal height 28 cm, fetal heart rate 142, no oedema
data_table.Investigations: OGTT 75 g: fasting 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hr 9.8 mmol/L, 2-hr 8.2 mmol/L; HbA1c 5.6%; urinalysis no glucose, no protein

lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation and management?

options.text: Diagnose overt diabetes in pregnancy (not GDM) based on the 1-hour value above 9.5 mmol/L and commence basal-bolus insulin therapy immediately, deferring lifestyle modification because pharmacotherapy is required from the outset
options.rationale: Overt diabetes in pregnancy requires fasting glucose at or above 7.0, random or 2-hour at or above 11.1, or HbA1c at or above 6.5 percent; her values do not meet these thresholds and she is not overtly diabetic. (Source: ADIPS Consensus 2017: Diagnosis of GDM)
source_refs: ADIPS Consensus 2017: Diagnosis of GDM

options.text: She does not meet diagnostic thresholds for gestational diabetes because all three OGTT values are below the WHO 2013 cut-offs of 5.5 fasting, 11.1 at 1-hour and 8.5 at 2-hour; reassure and repeat routine antenatal care
options.rationale: ADIPS Australian thresholds are lower than the WHO cut-offs quoted, and fasting of 5.3 already exceeds 5.1 mmol/L; the WHO 2013 criteria align closely with ADIPS rather than disagreeing. (Source: ADIPS Consensus 2017: Diagnosis of GDM)
source_refs: ADIPS Consensus 2017: Diagnosis of GDM

options.text: She meets ADIPS criteria for gestational diabetes mellitus on the basis of the elevated fasting glucose; commence self-monitoring of blood glucose, refer to a multidisciplinary diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic and provide medical nutrition therapy
options.rationale: ADIPS 2017 thresholds for GDM by 75 g OGTT: fasting at or above 5.1, 1-hour at or above 10.0, 2-hour at or above 8.5 mmol/L; her fasting 5.3 alone qualifies. Initial management is SMBG, dietitian and multidisciplinary clinic. (Source: ADIPS Consensus 2017: Diagnosis of GDM)
source_refs: ADIPS Consensus 2017: Diagnosis of GDM

options.text: Diagnose impaired glucose tolerance only and recommend a repeat 75 g OGTT in 4 weeks to confirm persistence before committing the woman to the multidisciplinary diabetes-in-pregnancy pathway with all of its associated lifestyle interventions
options.rationale: IGT as a pre-pregnancy concept does not apply during pregnancy; ADIPS uses GDM diagnosis with the lower thresholds and starts management on a single confirmed test. (Source: ADIPS Consensus 2017: Diagnosis of GDM)
source_refs: ADIPS Consensus 2017: Diagnosis of GDM

options.text: Recommend a 3-day high-carbohydrate diet and repeat the OGTT to confirm because false positives are common in women with high BMI and a single high fasting value during pregnancy is unreliable as a stand-alone test
options.rationale: Pre-test carbohydrate priming is not required; a single OGTT with any value above threshold establishes the diagnosis under ADIPS without repeat. (Source: ADIPS Consensus 2017: Diagnosis of GDM)
source_refs: ADIPS Consensus 2017: Diagnosis of GDM

explanation.summary: Australian Diabetes in Pregnancy Society (ADIPS) 2017 criteria diagnose gestational diabetes mellitus on a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test at 24 to 28 weeks (or earlier if high risk) with any one of: fasting plasma glucose at or above 5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour at or above 10.0 mmol/L, or 2-hour at or above 8.5 mmol/L. Overt diabetes in pregnancy is fasting at or above 7.0, random at or above 11.1 or HbA1c at or above 6.5 percent. Initial GDM management is medical nutrition therapy, blood glucose self-monitoring (fasting and 1- or 2-hour postprandials), exercise advice and multidisciplinary review. Metformin or insulin is added if glycaemic targets are not met after one to two weeks.
key_points: 75g OGTT thresholds: fasting 5.1, 1-hr 10.0, 2-hr 8.5 mmol/L
Any single value above threshold = GDM
Overt DM in pregnancy: FPG 7.0+, random 11.1+, HbA1c 6.5%+
First-line: diet, exercise, SMBG, dietitian
Targets: fasting under 5.0, 1-hr under 7.4, 2-hr under 6.7 mmol/L
explanation.pearls: Early OGTT (under 20 weeks) in high-risk women with previous GDM, BMI over 35, prior macrosomic baby or family history catches preexisting type 2 diabetes that would otherwise be misdiagnosed as GDM.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Diagnosis of gestational diabetes mellitus
